title: Pistachio and cranberry cantucci
subtitle: Twice-baked Italian biscuits with pistachios and dried cranberries
excerpt: Cantucci (also called biscotti di Prato) are twice-baked Italian biscuits: baked first as a log, sliced, then returned to the oven to dry out completely. The result is very hard and dry, made for dunking in Vin Santo or coffee. Pistachios and dried cranberries give a colour and flavour contrast.
sourceNotes: Cantucci di Prato are documented in Tuscan baking literature from the nineteenth century, with documented recipes appearing in public-domain Italian cookbooks. The twice-baked method is established in the public record. The pistachio and cranberry variation follows standard home-baking practice.

recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe and bake two logs on the same tray.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cantucci improve with age. Keeps in an airtight tin for 3 to 4 weeks. The texture continues to dry and harden over the first week.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan: 155 degrees C for first bake; reduce to 145 degrees C conventional (fan: 125 degrees C) for the second bake

glossaryTerms.definition: Cantucci are baked twice: the dough is shaped into a flat log and baked until cooked through but still soft enough to slice, then cut on the diagonal into individual biscuits and returned to a lower oven for 20 to 25 minutes to dry out completely. The second bake removes almost all remaining moisture, producing the characteristic dry, very hard texture that allows the biscuits to be kept for weeks and to absorb liquid (wine, coffee) without turning soggy immediately.

content.text: Cantucci are an exercise in patience: the dough is mixed, the log baked, allowed to cool briefly, sliced diagonally, and then returned to the oven for a second, longer bake. The

method produces a biscuit with almost no moisture in it. The pistachio and cranberry are added whole; the cranberries become slightly jammy in the first bake and the pistachios stay crunchy through both.

Preheat the oven to 175 degrees C (conventional). Line a baking tray with baking paper. Whisk together the flour, sugar, baking powder, and salt in a large bowl. In a separate bowl, beat the eggs with the vanilla and orange zest. Pour the wet ingredients into the dry and mix until a stiff dough forms. Fold in the pistachios and dried cranberries. The dough will be sticky. Divide in half and shape each portion into a flat log about 25 cm long and 8 cm wide on the prepared tray, leaving space between them. Bake for 25 to 30 minutes until the logs are golden and firm to the touch. Remove from the oven and leave to cool for 15 minutes.

Reduce the oven to 145 degrees C (conventional). Using a serrated knife, cut each log on the diagonal into 1.5 cm slices. Lay the slices flat on the baking tray. Return to the oven for 20 to 25 minutes, turning once at the halfway point, until the biscuits are dry and golden on both sides. They will still feel slightly soft in the centre when warm; they harden as they cool. Store in an airtight tin once completely cool.
